coredata.dc:title: Bifunctional strategy based on MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 nanocomposite photosensitive materials: Ultrasensitive detection of prostate antigens and preliminary design of a portable detector

coredata.dc:description: In this work, a novel photoelectrochemical (PEC) immunosensor based on molybdenum disulfide (MoS2)/nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (N,S-CQDs)/bismuth sulfide (Bi2S3) nanocomposites was designed for the quantitative detection of prostate-specific antigen (PSA) in human serum and urine. For the initial time, we used the synthesized N,S-CQDs as a Mo4 + and Bi3+ sulfur source. A one-step hydrothermal process was used to create highly photoactive MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 nanoheterojunctions. Compared to pure MoS2 and Bi2S3, MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 exhibited better photoelectric activity and electron transfer efficiency. By the layer-by-layer assembly method, the specificity between PSA and prostate-specific antibody (Anti-PSA) was used to form an immune complex, which increased the spatial site resistance of MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3, leading to a decrease in photocurrent transmission efficiency, thus reaching the purpose of direct detection of PSA concentration. Under the optimized conditions, the photoelectric signal intensity of the MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 PEC immunosensor showed a good linear relationship with the concentration of PSA in the range of 0.001 ng/mL to 50 ng/mL, and the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.35 pg/mL. Regarding quantitative PSA detection, the developed PEC immunosensor offers outstanding selectivity, sensitivity, and stability, which can provide an effective strategy for diagnosing and treating prostate cancer. In addition, to detect PSA and other biomarkers with speed and precision, a novel portable PEC detector model was proposed and designed in this study, and the construction of this model has great potential for application in clinical and other disease diagnosis.

(h) 10 ng/mL PSA + CEA (10 ng/mL), (i) PSA (10 ng/mL) + AFP (100 ng/mL). (j) PSA (10 ng/mL) + HSA (100 ng/mL), (k) PSA (10 ng/mL) + BSA (100 ng/mL), (l) PSA (10 ng/mL) + HIgG (100 ng/mL) (n = 3). The error bars depend on three parallel measurements.

head.ce:title: Bifunctional strategy based on MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 nanocomposite photosensitive materials: Ultrasensitive detection of prostate antigens and preliminary design of a portable detector

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a novel photoelectrochemical (PEC) immunosensor based on molybdenum disulfide (MoS2)/nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (N,S-CQDs)/bismuth sulfide (Bi2S3) nanocomposites was designed for the quantitative detection of prostate-specific antigen (PSA) in human serum and urine. For the initial time, we used the synthesized N,S-CQDs as a Mo4 + and Bi3+ sulfur source. A one-step hydrothermal process was used to create highly photoactive MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 nanoheterojunctions. Compared to pure MoS2 and Bi2S3, MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 exhibited better photoelectric activity and electron transfer efficiency. By the layer-by-layer assembly method, the specificity between PSA and prostate-specific antibody (Anti-PSA) was used to form an immune complex, which increased the spatial site resistance of MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3, leading to a decrease in photocurrent transmission efficiency, thus reaching the purpose of direct detection of PSA concentration. Under the optimized conditions, the photoelectric signal intensity of the MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 PEC immunosensor showed a good linear relationship with the concentration of PSA in the range of 0.001 ng/mL to 50 ng/mL, and the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.35 pg/mL. Regarding quantitative PSA detection, the developed PEC immunosensor offers outstanding selectivity, sensitivity, and stability, which can provide an effective strategy for diagnosing and treating prostate cancer. In addition, to detect PSA and other biomarkers with speed and precision, a novel portable PEC detector model was proposed and designed in this study, and the construction of this model has great potential for application in clinical and other disease diagnosis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, cancer has become one of the main causes of mortality for people throughout the world [1]. PSA is a glycoprotein produced by the prostate's ducts and epithelium. It is used as a biomarker for prostate cancer [2,3]. Its concentration in a healthy person's serum was typically less than 4.0 ng/mL, and the most accurate tumor marker for identifying the various stages of prostate cancer was an abnormal rise in serum PSA levels [4–6]. Consequently, the rapid and sensitive detection of PSA in human serum may provide an effective strategy for the diagnosis and treatment of prostate cancer.

So far, researchers have created various PSA measurement techniques, including mass spectrometry immunoassay [7,8], electrochemistry [9], and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [10,11]. Despite the maturity of these methods, they are still complex, expensive and have low sensitivity. In contrast to these methods, PEC immunoassays, which are based on specific reactions between antigen and matched antibodies exhibit superior characteristics such as simplicity of the device, low background interference, high sensitivity, high selectivity and good stability [12–14]. The essential of PEC detection is to convert light irradiation into an electrical signal using light as the excitation source, which provides excellent sensitivity by ignoring interference from background signals [15,16].

Nonetheless, choosing the right photoactive nanomaterials was crucial for building PEC immunosensors. As an important photoactive semiconductor material, Bi2S3 has a narrow band gap (1.3–1.7 eV) and was constantly used to sensitize other materials to regulate the band gap of the materials [17–19]. It has proven to be widely used in the construction of PEC sensors, biosensors, electrochemistry and photocatalysis. For instance, Cui et al. developed a PEC sensor platform for miRNA-21 detection utilizing Bi2S3 and CdIn2S4 [20]. Gao et al. used Bi2S3 with BiVO4/WO3 to construct photocatalysts for hydrogen evolution applications [21].

Recently, MoS2 nanomaterials, with large surface area, tunable energy band diagram (1.2–1.8 eV), fast electron mobility and photoluminescence, have played an important role in photochemical and electrochemical applications and have attracted a great deal of attention from researchers [22,23]. For instance, Li et al. reported a novel MoS2@ReS2/Ti3C2-based PEC biosensor for the ultrasensitive detection of piRNA-31143 [24]. Hu et al. constructed a novel PEC sensor based on MoS2/Au/GaN composites for the detection of cancer biomarker alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) [25]. Nevertheless, MoS2 has obvious shortcomings in practical applications, such as aggregation and lateral transfer of electrons due to van der Waals interactions between MoS2 layered crystals, both of which reduce the number of photoexcited active sites and the efficiency of electron transfer [26–28].

Additionally, CQDs, when combined with other semiconductor materials to form composites, have a closer contact and a more uniform distribution, which can accelerate the charge transfer and improve the photoelectric activity of other materials [29–31]. This is due to their small size, biocompatibility, and light-induced electron transfer. However, heteroatom-doped CQDs exhibit superior properties compared to pure CQDs. According to research, heteroatom doping significantly improves the optical properties, surface chemistry properties, and detection sensitivity of CQDs by generating extra p-type or n-type carriers along the valence or conduction bands [32–34]. Studies on composites such as BCQD/gC3N4 [35], SnO2/NCQDs/Bi2S3 [16], SnO2/NCQDs/BiOI [36], NCQDs/Bi4MoO9 [37], ZnO/CH3NH3PbI3/NCQDs [38] and CQDs/AuCQDs [39] have shown that heteroatom-doped CQDs combined with other semiconductor materials can enhance their photoelectric activity.

Inspired by the above, in this experiment, the synthesized N,S-CQDs not only serve as a charging bridge to facilitate charge separation and charge transfer for Mo4 + and Bi3+ co-sensitization but also provide a sulfur source for Mo4 + and Bi3+. The highly photoactive MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 heterojunctions were prepared by sequential addition of Mo4 + and Bi3+ to form homogeneous solutions with N,S-CQDs, followed by a one-step hydrothermal method. A layer-by-layer construction procedure was employed to build a PEC immunosensor for detecting PSA in human serum and urine using MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 as the basis material. This research offers a fresh approach to building a PEC immunosensor platform with great potential for clinical and other disease diagnostic applications. In addition, considering the complexity of operation and inconvenience of portability of traditional PEC workstations, this paper proposes and designs an integrated portable PEC detector for the detection of PSA and other biomarkers.
Detailed information on instruments and reagents can be found in the Supplementary Material.

Briefly, N,S-CQDs were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method. Before the experiment, L-cysteine (484 mg) and thiourea (460 mg) were added to ultrapure water to prepare a homogeneous aqueous solution (20 mL). After being moved into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, the aforementioned solution was heated to 200°C for 10 h. Lastly, a freeze-drying process was used to create a pure solid of N,S-CQDs from the obtained N,S-CQDs solution.

Firstly, sodium molybdate (0.7259 g) was dissolved in 25 mL of ultrapure water, transferred to a 100 mL conical flask and stirred magnetically for 3 h at a constant temperature of 40℃, then 15 mL of N,S-CQDs (10 mg/mL) was added and stirred for another 3 h to mix the sodium molybdate with the N,S-CQDs. Next bismuth nitrate (0.517 g) was added and stirred continuously for 3 h. Subsequently, the obtained homogeneous solution was put into a 50 mL autoclave lined with Teflon and gradually heated to 200°C for 20 h. After cooling to ambient temperature, the black suspension was centrifuged at 4200 rpm to obtain a black precipitate, which underwent three rounds of washing with ethanol and water before being dried for 12 h at 60°C in an oven and marked as MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3. Scheme 1 shows the process of synthesis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the novel PEC sensor
ce:section.ce:para: According to the layer-by-layer assembly method, the fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) conducting glass (2.5 ×2.5 cm2) was cleaned by consecutive ultrasonic cleaning with acetone, ethanol, and ultrapure water for 30 min before starting assembly, and then transferred to a 60°C oven for drying. The fabrication procedure of the novel PEC sensor was demonstrated in Scheme 2. At first, FTO conductive glass with an exposed geometric area of 0.64 cm2 was coated with 20 µL of MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 (10 mg/mL) suspension and dried in an oven at 200°C for 12 h, with the resulting electrode was labeled as FTO/MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3. Subsequently, 5 μL of 10 µmol/L thioglycolic acid (TGA) was added dropwise to the FTO/MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 electrode for 30 min and then washed with phosphate buffered saline (PBS) with pH of 7.2–7.4. Then, 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 10 µmol/L) / N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 2 µmol/L) mixed solution (5 μL) was modified on an FTO/MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3/TGA electrode for 60 min to activate -COOH of TGA and the redundant TGA were washed off to obtain an FTO/MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3/TGA/(EDC/NHS) electrode. After that, 10 µL of Anti-PSA solution with a concentration of 10 μg/mL was further modified and labeled as FTO/MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3/TGA/(EDC/NHS)/Anti-PSA. Subsequently, After 10 µL of the bovine serum albumin (BSA, 10 ng/mL) was applied to the modified electrode, it reacted for 60 min at room temperature to block nonspecific sites. The resulting electrode was labeled as FTO/MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3/TGA/(EDC/NHS)/Anti-PSA/BSA. At last, the PSA solution (5 μL) at various concentrations was incubated for 60 min at room temperature with the FTO/MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3/TGA/(EDC/NHS)/Anti-PSA/BSA electrodes. The electrodes were then washed with ultrapure water and kept at 4°C for further photocurrent measurements.

The quantification of PSA in samples with different concentrations was performed using FTO electrodes with MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 composites attached according to the specific binding of antigens and antibodies. The photocurrent study used a CHI-660E electrochemical workstation with a three-electrode system, which included an Ag/AgCl electrode for the reference electrode, and a platinum wire electrode for the counter electrode. The FTO sensor attached with MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 composite materials was used as the working electrode. The light source throughout the experiment was a 250 W xenon lamp, and the applied voltage was set to 0 V.

To evaluate the potential of the novel aptamer sensor in real sample analysis, PSA was assayed in human serum and urine samples. Fresh human blood and urine samples were centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 20 min to obtain pure serum and urine samples, the supernatant was filtered through a nylon filter (0.45 μm), diluted 100-fold with pure water, and set aside. To investigate the recovery, diluted serum and urine samples were mixed with low, medium, and high concentrations of PSA solutions. and analyzed by the standard spiking method (The human serum and urine samples of this experiment came from Jilin University’s First Affiliated Hospital and were authorized by the ethics committee).

The N,S-CQDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). As shown in Fig. 1A, the morphology of the synthesized N,S-CQDs was close to spherical shape with uniform size distribution. The inset shows that the aqueous solution of N,S-CQDs appears yellow under natural light and blue under 365 nm UV irradiation, which indicates that the synthesized N,S-CQDs have a certain absorption ability to visible light. The average particle size of N,S-CQDs was calculated to be 5.95 ± 1.24 nm (Fig. 1B), and its size was favorable for the reaction and binding with Mo4+ and Bi3+. A large absorption band at 3000–3300 cm−1, which is connected to the stretching vibration of O-H and N-H, can be seen in the FT-IR spectral characterization displayed in Fig. 1C [40]. This suggests that N,S-CQDs are very hydrophilic and contain more amino and hydroxyl groups. The absorption peak located at 2053.8 cm−1 was ascribed to stretching vibrations of the C-N bond [41]. Conjugated structure stretching vibrational absorption peaks at CC and CN were identified as the source of the peak at 1649.91 cm−1 [40]. The peak at 1401.25 cm−1 is attributed to the bending vibration of CN. The peaks of CS/SO and C-S stretching vibrations were located at 1102.54 and 611.66 cm−1, respectively. The aforementioned outcomes validate the efficient doping of nitrogen and sulfur elements into CQDs, confirming the successful synthesis of N,S-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural analysis of the prepared materials
ce:section.ce:para: The morphological characteristics of MoS2, Bi2S3 and MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 materials were obtained by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) analysis. As shown in Fig. 2A, the synthesized N,S-CQDs were used as a sulfur source in combination with Mo4+ to synthesize MoS2 nanomaterials. The synthesized MoS2 showed a spherical flower-like structure, which is consistent with literature reports [42], proving the successful synthesis of MoS2 nanomaterials. Using a similar method as MoS2, the synthesized N,S-CQDs were reacted with Bi3+ to obtain rod-like Bi2S3 nanomaterials (Fig. 2B), and the results were similar to the study by Jiang et al. [43], further demonstrating that N,S-CQDs can be used as good sulfur source materials. Based on the above study, a one-step hydrothermal solvent method was used to construct MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 nanocomposites by the reaction of N,S-CQDs with Mo4+ and Bi3+ as shown in Fig. 2C, from which it can be observed that not only the Bi2S3 nanorods but also N,S-CQDs existed on the surface of MoS2 spherical nanomaterials and their surroundings. The reason for this phenomenon may be that the addition of N,S-CQDs is excessive, and when the reaction with Mo4+ and Bi3+ is complete there is still a small amount of N,S-CQDs remaining attached to the surface and around MoS2 and Bi2S3. The TEM characterization of the MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 composites is shown in Fig. 2D, from which MoS2 nanospheres and Bi2S3 nanorods can be observed, which is in good agreement with the above SEM observations. From Fig. 2E-F high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), it can be seen that the lattice stripe of the spherical flower-like MoS2 nanospheres is 0.652 nm (002). The lattice stripe of rod-shaped Bi2S3 was 0.372 nm (101), and all these results confirm that N,S-CQDs can provide sulfur sources for Mo4+ and Bi3+. In addition, the elemental composition and content ratio of the MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 composite surface were investigated. Fig. 3A shows the SEM image of the MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 composites, and Figs. 3B and 3C show the energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) corresponding to the MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 composites, respectively. The above characterization indicates the successful formation of MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 photoactive composites.

To better characterize the elemental composition and valence of MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 nanocomposites, an in-depth analysis was performed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. 4A, the full-scan XPS spectrum of MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 was measured, and the results indicated that the MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 contained mainly C, O, N, S, Mo, and Bi elements, which were consistent with EDS. Fig. 4B displays the XPS results for Bi 4f and S 2p. It shows two fitted peaks at 158.6 and 163.9 eV that are connected to 4f 7/2 and 4f 5/2 of Bi3+, respectively. S 2p is responsible for the weak peak at 161.4 eV that lies in between the two main peaks [44]. Fig. 4C shows a correlation between the binding energies of 232.6 and 235.3 eV with the spin-orbit splitting of Mo4+ 3d orbital (3d 5/2 and 3d 3/2, respectively). In addition, the 225.6 eV fitted peak can be attributed to S 2 s [45]. Based on these results, the successful synthesis of MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 was further confirmed.

To further investigate the electron transport efficiency of the synthesized materials, steady-state photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopic characterization was performed as shown in Fig. 4D. The complexation rate of photogenerated charges affects the intensity of PL; the more significant the separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, the lower the PL intensity [46]. The PL intensity of MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 is significantly lower than that of N,S-CQDs, MoS2, and Bi2S3. This could be because of the effective separation and transportation of photogenerated charges brought about by the fabrication of MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 photoactive materials. Fig. 4E show the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of MoS2, Bi2S3, and MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3. It can be seen that N,S-CQDs react with Bi3+ to form Bi2S3 at 15.8°, 17.6°, 24.9°, 28.6°, 31.8°, 46.5°, 52.6°, corresponding to (020), (120), (130), (211), (221), (431), and (351) planes, respectively (JCPDS No.17–0320) [47], which indicates that N,S-CQDs can provide a sulfur source to successfully react with Bi3+ and synthesize Bi2S3. Three characteristic diffraction peaks can be found in the pattern of MoS2 formed by reacting N,S-CQDs with Mo4+ at 10.3°, 32.7° and 58.3°, which are consistent with (002), (100) and (110) planes (JCPDS No. 37–1492) [48], similar to the synthesis of Bi2S3, and it can also indicate that N,S-CQDs can successfully react with Mo4+ and synthesize MoS2 [49]. It was noted that there was a change in the peak on the (002) crystal plane relative to the usual MoS2 (002) crystal plane, which could be attributed to oxygen doping. The characteristic diffraction peaks of Bi2S3 and part of MoS2 are observed in the MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 composites obtained by combining Mo4+, N,S-CQDs and Bi3+. The phenomena may be caused by MoS2’s low crystallinity, which results in the distinctive diffraction peaks of Bi2S3 covering those of MoS2. In addition, the crystal structure of the prepared samples did not change significantly, which indicates that the MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 photoactive materials were successfully constructed and well dispersed. Meanwhile, the ultraviolet-visible diffuse reflectance (UV–vis) spectra of MoS2, Bi2S3, and MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 are depicted in Fig. 4F. Among them, the MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 composites have increased absorption of visible light compared with MoS2 and Bi2S3, indicating that the use of N,S-CQDs as intermediates can improve the absorption of visible light by MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance study of the fabricated new PEC sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The PEC properties of MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 composites were investigated utilizing photocurrent-time curves and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests. The modified FTO electrode’s photocurrent-time curves in a PBS with ascorbic acid (AA, 0.1 mol/L) are displayed in Fig. 5A. Nearly 0 µA was the photocurrent of the bare FTO electrode. And the photocurrent of the modified FTO/MoS2 and FTO/Bi2S3 increased. However, the composites of FTO/MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 constructed with N,S-CQDs as intermediates showed a strong photocurrent signal (200 µA) compared to MoS2 and Bi2S3. It may be deduced that N,S-CQDs built as sulfur sources for MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 composites can encourage the creation of electron-hole pairs, resulting in composites with high visible light activity, and this conclusion was supported by the UV–vis as mentioned above analysis. Therefore, FTO/MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 electrodes will be used to construct the subsequent PEC sensors. By observing the photocurrent response, the immunosensor's assembling procedure was examined. As shown in Fig. 5B, the MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 composite was decorated on the surface of the FTO electrode to obtain a strong PEC signal (curve b), which proved that the composite had an excellent PEC performance. The PEC signal was gradually weakened with the layer-by-layer assembly of TGA, EDC/NHS, Anti-PSA, BSA, and PSA (curves c to g). Additionally, as illustrated in Fig. 5C (where the Randles equivalent circuit simulated using ZView software is inset), the successful establishment of the PEC sensor was further confirmed by examining the electrodes' EIS, and the electrode transfer resistance (Ret), which can vary depending on the electrode modifications, and can be effectively described by the EIS. This technique is frequently employed to examine the sequential assembly of the PEC immunosensor process. The semicircle diameter in the EIS plot reflects the Ret value, and the blank electrode diameter (curve a) corresponds to a smaller value of Ret. The semicircular diameters gradually became more significant with the modification of MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 (curve b). The electrodes were modified with TGA and EDC/NHS on this basis, where the surface of MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 contains amino and carboxyl groups that can effectively bind with TGA due to its insulating properties, leading to a further increase (curve c). Next, activation of the carboxyl group of TGA with EDC/NHS promoted better binding of the carboxyl-functionalized TGA to Anti-PSA, and its insulating quality also caused the electrode’s semicircular diameter to rise (curve d). At the same time, when compared to the materials mentioned above, the impending impact of altering the electrode surface with macromolecular protein compounds like Anti-PSA, BSA, and PSA was more noticeable and resulted in a considerable rise in the Ret value (curves e-g). The reason is that protein molecules have a high spatial site resistance, which prevents electrons and holes from diffusing to the electrode surface. It suggests that every modification made to the PEC immunosensor during fabrication was successful. The PEC immunosensor was successfully constructed, according to the findings above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electron transfer mechanism of MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 PEC sensors
ce:section.ce:para:
The PEC sensor's electron transfer mechanism was examined in a PBS electrolyte that contained AA. Using the Kubelka-Munk theory, the band gap energy (Eg) and absorption coefficient α were determined by calculating the relationship between (αhν)2 and the light energy (hν). The following is the formula [50]:
α h v
2
∝ h v − E g
Where ν is the frequency of light, α is the absorption coefficient, and h is Planck's constant. The findings of the Tauc plot approach, with hv as the horizontal coordinate and (αhν)2 as the vertical coordinate, are displayed in Fig. S1 (A-C). The Eg energies of MoS2, N,S-CQDs, and Bi2S3 can be obtained as 1.7 eV, 1.51 eV, and 1.47 eV, respectively, which are approximately the same as previously reported [43,51].

The next step was to obtain the flat-band potentials of MoS2, N,S-CQDs and Bi2S3 based on the Mott-Schottky (M-S) test to demonstrate the charge transfer mechanism of MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3. The results are shown in Fig. S1 (D-F), showing that the slopes of the three curves were all positive, indicating that the three single materials all have the characteristics of typical n-type semiconductors. The M-S curves show that the flat band potentials of MoS2, N,S-CQDs and Bi2S3 were −0.27 eV, −0.25 eV and −0.21 eV, respectively, relative to AgCl electrodes. The flat-band potential of the material relative to the normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) was calculated using Formula E (NHE)= E (Ag/AgCl) + 0.197 V. This resulted in values of 0.073 eV, −0.053 eV, and −0.013 eV. According to the reported literature [52], it was known that for n-type semiconductors, the flat-band potential is 0.2 eV higher than the valence-band potential (E VB). Therefore, it can be derived that the E VB of MoS2, N,S-CQDs and Bi2S3 were −0.273 eV, −0.253 eV, and −0.213 eV, respectively. According to the formula Eg = E VB- E CB [53], the conduction band potentials (E CB) of MoS2, N,S-CQDs and Bi2S3 were calculated to be −1.973 eV, −1.763 eV and −1.683 eV, respectively. Based on the above results, the electron transport paths based on the potentials of MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 composites were obtained (Fig. 5D). It can be visualized that the energy bands of the composites have a step-like structure, which is advantageous for the flow and separation of holes and electrons. Moreover, semiconductor materials such as MoS2, N,S-CQD and Bi2S3 have a narrow bandgap, making it easier to separate light-generated electrons and holes. It is again shown that MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 composites obtained by a one-step hydrothermal method using N,S-CQDs as the sulfur source with Mo4+ and Bi3+ have good absorption of visible light. Visible light stimulated the MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 composites, causing light-generated electrons to migrate from MoS2’s valence band (VB) to its conduction band (CB). The electrons are stimulated to move quickly between the CB of MoS2, N,S-CQDs, and Bi2S3 when exposed to visible light, and they eventually go to the FTO surface. It is also remarkable that MoS2, N,S-CQDs, and Bi2S3 all have progressively increasing CB and VB edges that facilitate the transmission of photogenerated carriers. Meanwhile, AA is an outstanding electron donor, which hinders the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes and facilitates the transfer of electrons from CB to FTO, leading to an increase in photocurrent signal. Thus, PEC sensors that operate superbly in the presence of visible light were effectively built.

The experiments mainly studied the volume of N,S-CQDs, the drop sample volume of MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3, the effect of AA concentration and pH on the photocurrent signal of FTO/MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 electrode.

As shown in Fig. 6A, with the increase of the dosage of N,S-CQDs, the photocurrent intensity showed a trend of rising and then decreasing, and the best photoelectric signal at 15 mL, presumably because when the sulfur element in the added N,S-CQDs fully combined with the molybdenum and bismuth elements to generate MoS2 and Bi2S3, the excessive N,S-CQDs might hinder the electron transfer. This leads to a decrease in the photogenerated electron-hole production rate. To investigate the effect of MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 dosage on the photocurrent signal, different volumes of MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 were added dropwise to the FTO electrode sheet, and the results shown in Fig. 6B were obtained. It can be seen that when the volume of MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 solution is 20 μL, the photocurrent signal reaches the maximum value, and then there is a decreasing trend, which is mainly due to the decrease of the visible light absorption ability of MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 when the volume is larger than 20 μL, which leads to a decrease in the photocurrent signal. In addition, AA is a good electron donor, which hinders the recombination of photogenerated e-/h+ pairs and thus accelerates the photogenerated carrier transport. As shown in Fig. 6C, the photoelectric signal was gradually enhanced with the increase of AA concentration in the range of 0–0.1 mol/L. The photocurrent signal progressively decreases as the concentration increases from 0.1 mol/L to 1.0 mol/L. When the AA concentration is higher than 0.1 mol/L, the electron donor saturates, which is why the highest value was attained at 0.1 mol/L AA. Thus, it was discovered that 0.1 moL/L was the optimal AA concentration. Additionally, Fig. 6D demonstrated that the photoelectric signal increased in the pH range of 4–5.5. However, the photoelectric signal did not show a significant change when the pH was higher than 5.5. Considering that the human physiological pH was between 7.35 and 7.45, which was suitable for fixing protein activity, PBS (7.2–7.4) was chosen for subsequent experimental studies in this experiment.

Based on the results of the above study, the innovative PEC electrodes modified with various PSA concentrations were studied under optimum conditions. From the photocurrent curves ( Fig. 7A), with the specific binding of Anti-PSA to different concentrations of PSA, the photocurrent response on the PEC sensor decreased with increasing PSA concentration. The main reason was that PSA blocks electron transfer on the PEC electrode surface by accumulating immune complexes with Anti-PSA to produce an inert barrier layer. This further indicated the successful preparation of a label-free PEC sensor that can detect PSA. Additionally, there was a good linear relationship between photocurrent intensity and PSA concentration between 0.001 ng/mL and 50 ng/mL (Fig. 7B), and the fitted linear equation was I = -4.822logC PSA(ng/mL) + 19.03 (r 2 = 0.9916), and the LOD was 0.35 pg/mL according to the calculation method in the literature [54]. Relative to the majority of previously published PSA assays (Table S1, Supplementary material), the more pleasing linear range and low detection limit are features of the PEC immunosensor designed in this investigation, demonstrating that the PEC immunosensor has a promising application and potential for the rapid detection of other tumor markers in human serum and urine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity, stability and interference of PEC immunosensors
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the PEC immunosensor's stability, we recorded the photocurrent response of the PEC immunosensor modified with the addition of 10 ng/mL PSA for 400 s under 10 on/off illumination cycles in this work (Fig. 7C). It was observed from the figure that the photocurrent did not change significantly with time, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) of PEC measurement was 1.9 %, indicating that the prepared PEC immunosensor has good stability. Specificity and interfering properties are also important criteria for examining PEC immunosensor assays. To prove that the specific binding of PSA and Anti-PSA is the source of the photocurrent response, some representative interfering proteins, including human serum albumin (HSA), human immunoglobulin G (HIgG), AFP, BSA, and carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), were selected for selectivity and interference testing (Fig. 7D). These interfering proteins did not cause significant photocurrent signal changes, indicating that the PSA assay has satisfactory selectivity and resistance to interference.

Generally, in serum from healthy adults, the PSA concentration is less than 4.0 ng/mL. To show that the PEC biosensor can be used to measure PSA in actual human serum, the recovery of different concentrations of PSA in serum samples was measured by the standard addition method. The results are visualized in Table S2, where the RSD was less than 1.54 % and the spiked recoveries ranged from 97.4 % to 103.0 %. The results showed that the biosensor has the potential to determine the PSA in real serum sample analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preliminary design of a portable PEC detector
ce:section.ce:para: The traditional PEC workstation mainly consists of electrochemical units, electrochemical cells, electrodes, reference electrodes, electrochemical probes, metrology equipment, as well as a variety of controllers and software, and thus constitutes a large PEC workstation instrument (Fig. S2A), which has the disadvantages of difficulty in portability and detection lag. Based on this, a portable PEC detector (Fig. S2B, Fig. 8A-F) was initially designed in this study, which can make up for the shortcomings of the traditional PEC workstation, providing a good detection method and means for the subsequent detection of PSA and other biomarkers, and have great prospects for the diagnosis of diseases and other clinical applications. Detailed information on portable PEC detectors can be found in the Supplementary material.

This work used L-cysteine and thiourea as raw ingredients to create a new type of N,S-CQDs employing a one-step hydrothermal solvent technique for the first time, and a new PEC immunosensor of MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 nanocomposite was constructed by a one-step hydrothermal solvent method combining N,S-CQDs as a sulfur source with molybdenum and bismuth elements again. With the use of FT-IR, SEM, EDS, TEM, and XPS, the size, shape, atomic composition, and optoelectronic characteristics of MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 nanocomposites were examined, and the successful synthesis of MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3 was confirmed. It was also used for the quantitative detection of PSA in human serum and urine. By the layer-by-layer assembly method, the specificity between PSA antigen and PSA antibody was exploited to form an immune complex, which increased the spatial potential resistance of MoS2/N,S-CQDs/Bi2S3, resulting in a decrease in photocurrent transmission efficiency, thus achieving the direct detection of PSA concentration. The quantitative detection of PSA by this PEC immunosensor has a lower detection limit, good selectivity, sensitivity and stability compared with most reported methods. In addition, this study proposed and designed an all-in-one portable PEC detector. Currently, the detector needs to be continuously improved in terms of application. While there are still challenges to overcome for commercial application, the research idea and preliminary design can provide valuable research ideas for future scholars in this field.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yongxue Li completed her Master's degree in clinical pharmacy at the Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, People’s Republic of China, in 2023. His work focuses on fluorescence sensing, on the design of carbon dots, portable detection and visualization research, semiconductor material and photoelectrochemical biosensor.

Runuo Wang will complete her Master's degree in pharmaceutical analysis at Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, People’s Republic of China, in 2024. Her work focuses on colormetric sensing, on the design of carbon dots based on deep eutectic solvent, biomimetic catalysis, hydrogel material and photoelectrochemical biosensor.

Chunling Mao completed her Doctoral degree in pharmaceutical analysis at the Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, People’s Republic of China, in 2026. Her work focuses on fluorescence sensing, on the design of carbon dots, and photoelectrochemical biosensor.

Qian Hang will complete her Doctoral degree in pharmaceutical analysis at Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, People's Republic of China, in 2026. Her work focuses on fluorescence sensing, on the design of carbon dots based on deep eutectic solvent, biomimetic catalysis, hydrogel material and photoelectrochemical biosensor.

Longshan Zhao is Professor in pharmaceutical analysis at Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, People’s Republic of China. He obtained his PhD in pharmaceutical analysis at the Shenyang Pharmaceutical University in 2013. His research activities focus on the theoretical study of fluorescence sensing, biomimetic catalysis, portable detection and visualization research, electrochemical sensing, photocatalysis, deep eutectic solvent, metabolomics.
